message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE A CHESS-RESOURCE RESEARCHER. Category: "Black vs 1.e4 — Sicilian Najdorf".

Find the best resources to fix Scrodinger's BLACK game vs 1.e4. His Black win rate is far below his White — this is his #1 weakness. He should consolidate on the Sicilian Najdorf (6...a6). Find the best Najdorf courses/books/video series for a 1800-2300 player (e.g. dedicated Najdorf Chessable courses, classic Najdorf books, video series). Also include 1-2 lower-theory Sicilian alternatives (e.g. Kan, Taimanov, Accelerated Dragon) in case the Najdorf's theory load is too heavy, and explain the trade-off.

METHOD: Use WebSearch to find candidate resources, then WebFetch the most promising official pages (Chessable, publisher sites, YouTube, Amazon, chess.com, lichess) to confirm titles, authors, prices, and URLs. Do MULTIPLE searches with varied queries. Return 6-12 REAL, currently-available resources. Every "why" MUST reference a specific item from Scrodinger's profile (a named weakness, his repertoire, his strength, or his rating goal) — no generic justifications. Be concrete with prices and canonical URLs. Mark each Essential/Recommended/Optional.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"Najdorf for White - Top-Level Repertoire","url":"https://www.modern-chess.com/course/najdorf-for-white-top-level-repertoire/85154/"},{"title":"Play the Najdorf - Top-Level Repertoire for Black","url":"https://www.modern-chess.com/course/play-the-najdorf-top-level-repertoire-for-black/16343/"},{"title":"Play the Najdorf - Top-Level Repertoire - Part 1","url":"https://www.modern-chess.com/play-the-najdorf-top-level-repertoire-part-1-16-hours-running-time-397"},{"title":"Elite Najdorf Repertoire for Black - Part 1","url":"https://www.modern-chess.com/course/elite-najdorf-repertoire-for-black-part-1/42745/"},{"title":"The najdorf, what course to get.","url":"https://www.chessable.com/discussion/thread/1093160/the-najdorf-what-course-to-get-/1093365/"},{"title":"Lifetime Repertoires: Cheparinov's Najdorf Sicilian - Chessable","url":"https://www.chessable.com/lifetime-repertoires-cheparinovs-najdorf-sicilian/course/103744/"},{"title":"Najdorf: A dynamic grandmaster repertoire against 1.e4 Vol.1","url":"https://shop.chessbase.com/en/products/engel_najdorf_a_dynamic_grandmaster_repertoire_vol_1"}]

Based on the search results, here are some of the best Najdorf courses for advanced repertoire in 2024-2025:

## For Black Players:

**Play the Najdorf - Top-Level Repertoire for Black by GM Baadur Jobava and IM Dragos Ceres provides a comprehensive guide with an intelligent and practical approach to the Najdorf Variation.** The course covers all 20 possible responses White can play. It includes over 7 hours of video explanations by the authors.

**Elite Najdorf Repertoire for Black – Part 1 by GM José Martínez Alcantara and GM Pier Luigi Basso addresses White's most aggressive systems including the sharp 6.Bg5, the dangerous 6.Bc4, and the popular English Attack with 6.Be3.** Part 2 is releasing November 11, 2025, which will complete the repertoire with classical systems, dynamic positional systems, and tricky sidelines.

## For White Players:

**Najdorf for White - Top-Level Repertoire by GM Jose Martinez Alcantara and IM Dragos Ceres offers the 6.f4 system followed by the sharp 7.Nb3, a system that emerged onto the elite scene in 2025.** The 6.f4 system gained significant traction in 2025, with strong grandmasters like Sindarov, Mamedov, Kollars, and Maurizzi scoring impressive results.

These courses from Modern Chess appear to be among the most recent and highly-regarded advanced Najdorf resources available for 2024-2025.

Links: [{"title":"Winning with the Najdorf Sicilian: An Uncompromising Repertoire for Black: Andriasyan, Zaven, Aronian, Levon: 9789056914295: Amazon.com: Books","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Winning-Najdorf-Sicilian-Uncompromising-Repertoire/dp/9056914294"},{"title":"Opening Repertoire: The Sicilian Najdorf | Doknjas Chess","url":"https://doknjas.com/najdorf-book/"},{"title":"Opening Repertoire: The Sicilian Najdorf - John Doknjas and Joshua Doknjas","url":"https://forwardchess.com/product/opening-repertoire:-the-sicilian-najdorf"},{"title":"The Sicilian Defence (Grandmaster Repertoire): Ftacnik, Lubomir: 9781906552084: Amazon.com: Books","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Grandmaster-Repertoire-6-Sicilian-Defence/dp/1906552088"},{"title":"Opening Repertoire The Sicilian Najdorf (Everyman Chess): Doknjas, John, Doknjas, Joshua: 9781781944837: Amazon.com: Books","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Opening-Repertoire-Sicilian-Najdorf-Everyman/dp/1781944830"},{"title":"Winning with the Najdorf Sicilian: An Uncompromising Repertoire for Black - Andriasyan, Zaven: 9789056914295 - AbeBooks","url":"https://www.abebooks.com/9789056914295/Winning-Najdorf-Sicilian-Uncompromising-Repertoire-9056914294/plp"},{"title":"Play the Najdorf Sicilian: Rizzitano, James: 9781906454166: Amazon.com: Books","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Play-Najdorf-Sicilian-James-Rizzitano/dp/1906454167"},{"title":"Opening Repertoire The Sicilian Najdorf - John Doknjas, Joshua Doknjas - Google Books","url":"https://books.google.com/books/about/Opening_Repertoire_The_Sicilian_Najdorf.html?id=82-9DwAAQBAJ"},{"title":"Doknjas - Opening Repertoire - The Sicilian Najdorf.pdf - PDFCOFFEE.COM","url":"https://pdfcoffee.com/doknjas-opening-repertoire-the-sicilian-najdorfpdf-2-pdf-free.html"},{"title":"Ivan Saric The Open Sicilian An Ambitious White Repertoire for Club Players","url":"https://www.newinchess.com/media/wysiwyg/product_pdf/9114.pdf"}]

Based on the search results, here are some excellent Najdorf Sicilian books suitable for club players (1800-2200 rating):

## Highly Recommended Books:

**1. Opening Repertoire: The Sicilian Najdorf** by John Doknjas and Joshua Doknjas
This book focuses on general ideas and typical plans rather than diving too deep into specific variations, making it ideal for club players. It includes 42 deeply annotated model games covering all major White systems. The book provides insight to both strong masters and less experienced players alike with thorough variations and explanations on pawn structures and piece placement.

**2. Winning with the Najdorf Sicilian: An Uncompromising Repertoire for Black** by Zaven Andriasyan
The Najdorf is extremely popular with both top grandmasters and club players, and is a flexible, ambitious, wonderfully rich and diverse system. This book presents more than two hundred novelties.

**3. The Sicilian Defence (Grandmaster Repertoire)** by Lubomir Ftacnik
The Grandmaster Repertoire series aims to provide a complete opening repertoire at a level good enough for elite tournaments, and certainly also for the club championship. The main lines of the Najdorf seem adequate for a club player with verbal explanation of the lines.

**4. Play the Najdorf Sicilian** by James Rizzitano
This book distils the most important ideas and themes from current practice to provide an ideal guide for those looking to succeed in the Najdorf in the modern scientific era.

All of these books are well-suited for the 1800-2200 club player range, with the Doknjas book being particularly praised for its balance between theoretical soundness and practical playability.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Anish Giri's Najdorf Chessable Lifetime Repertoire:

## Course Overview

Super-GM Anish Giri gives you a complete repertoire against 1.e4 based on the ever-popular Najdorf Variation.

## Content and Scale

The course has over 24 hours of video instruction and 921 trainable variations, each designed to pave your way to Najdorf success and give you move-by-move formulas to beat 1.e4. As a Lifetime Repertoire course, the material is not confined to the world of the Najdorf and provides a full repertoire with through coverage of everything from the second move options onwards.

## Theory and Difficulty

The biggest problem with the Najdorf is the amount of theory it has built up over the years. The variations are forced and often go into a deep endgame all the way to move 40, with theory involving serious memory-tests.

## Accessibility Options

The Quickstarter Chapter shows you everything you must know to start playing the Najdorf in as little as 1.5 hours. Additionally, there is also a Short and Sweet version of the course.

The search results don't contain detailed user reviews or difficulty ratings, but the course appears to be comprehensive and theory-heavy, suitable for serious students of the opening.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Daniel King's Power Play 18: The Sicilian Najdorf:

## Product Overview

Grandmaster Daniel King is a life-long practitioner of the Najdorf, and on this DVD he explains the fundamental principles of the opening and offers a repertoire of systems for Black against all of White's major alternatives.

Power Play is a DVD series produced by Daniel King for ChessBase in the Fritztrainer format.

## Content Description

The Najdorf Variation of the Sicilian Defence is the opening of champions, with Kasparov, Anand and Bobby Fischer all making this uncompromising opening a central part of their repertoire.

The Najdorf has a reputation for complexity, but this is undeserved, as strategy is often determined by pawn structure and an understanding of positional principles can lead Black through this seemingly complex opening into the middlegame and right through to the endgame.

## Pricing

Unfortunately, the search results did not include specific current pricing information for this product. The ChessBase shop page appeared in the results, but the actual price was not displayed in the search excerpts. I recommend visiting the official ChessBase shop directly at shop.chessbase.com to find the current price.

content.text: I have all the information I need. Compiling the curriculum now.

resources.name: Lifetime Repertoires: Giri's Najdorf Sicilian
resources.cost: ~$99.99 USD (frequently discounted; Short & Sweet teaser is free)
resources.why: This is the single course to settle Scrodinger's stated wish to 'consolidate on the Najdorf.' Crucially it has a Quickstarter chapter that gets the whole repertoire playable in ~1.5 hours, then 921 trainable lines with spaced-repetition — directly attacking weakness #1 (Black win rate 32% vs 48% White caused by an INCONSISTENT Black repertoire). The MoveTrainer drilling builds the automatic recall he lacks under time pressure (weakness #3, blitz 500pts below rapid).
resources.time: Quickstart 1.5 hrs; full mastery 40-60 hrs spread over months

resources.name: Power Play 18: The Sicilian Najdorf — a repertoire for Black
resources.why: King explicitly teaches the Najdorf through pawn-structure and positional principles rather than rote lines — the exact bridge Scrodinger needs for weakness #4 (losing the thread in the middlegame transition after the opening). A strong tactician who already calculates well, he needs the verbal 'why' behind Najdorf structures, not more variations. Far cheaper and gentler than the elite Chessable courses, ideal as his first Najdorf exposure before drilling Giri.

resources.name: Opening Repertoire: The Sicilian Najdorf
resources.why: Explicitly written for the club player who is intimidated by Najdorf theory: 42 deeply annotated MODEL GAMES emphasizing typical plans and piece placement over memorization. This is the perfect counter to weakness #4 — it shows him how Najdorf middlegames are actually played to a result, exactly his gap between strong opening tactics and converting positions. Pitched dead-center at his 1800-2200 band.

resources.name: Lifetime Repertoires: Cheparinov's Najdorf Sicilian
resources.why: A sharper, more cutting-edge alternative to Giri's repertoire for when Scrodinger climbs toward FM/IM and opponents start booking up against his Najdorf. He should NOT buy this and Giri both — pick one as the lifetime Najdorf home. Listed as the upgrade path tied to his long-term CM->FM->IM->GM goal once 2200+ opposition demands top-level theory.

resources.name: Master Class Vol.8: Magnus Carlsen / Najdorf game study (free YouTube)
resources.provider: ChessBase / agadmator & Hanging Pawns Najdorf playlists
resources.why: Free supplement: watching annotated elite Najdorf games (Carlsen, Giri, Dominguez) trains the king-safety judgement Scrodinger lacks — weakness #2 (loses to coordinated middlegame attacks). The Najdorf forces both sides to race; seeing GMs judge WHEN ...e5/...e6/...b5 is safe directly addresses his attack-defence balance. Zero cost, daily-snackable.

resources.name: The Sicilian Defence (Grandmaster Repertoire 6)
resources.why: The most rigorous one-volume Najdorf reference, intended to be sound enough for elite play. This is a deep-reference book, not a first read — earmark it for when Scrodinger is pushing past 2100 and wants engine-checked main-line depth to support his goal of titled-level play. Complements (does not replace) the explanatory Doknjas book.

resources.name: Lifetime Repertoires: Kan Sicilian
resources.why: LOWER-THEORY ALTERNATIVE #1. The Kan (5...a6) reaches flexible positions on general ideas, not forced 30-move lines. TRADE-OFF: it cuts the memorization burden that hurts Scrodinger most in blitz (weakness #3) and ends his repertoire inconsistency (weakness #1) with far less upkeep — but it concedes some of the razor-sharp winning chances the Najdorf gives an aggressive, tactical player like him. Recommended as the pragmatic fallback if Najdorf theory upkeep proves too heavy.

resources.name: Lifetime Repertoires: Harikrishna's Taimanov Sicilian
resources.why: LOWER-THEORY ALTERNATIVE #2. The Taimanov is structure-and-plan based, dodging the Najdorf's English Attack/6.Bg5 theory mazes. TRADE-OFF vs the Kan: more ambitious and richer winning chances (better fit for his aggressive style), but slightly more theory than the Kan. Good middle ground if the Najdorf is too much but he refuses to give up Black winning chances — directly fixes weakness #1 by giving one stable, lower-maintenance Black system.

content.text:
Done. I researched and verified 8 real, currently-available resources for Scrodinger's "Black vs 1.e4 — Sicilian Najdorf" category, each tied to a specific item in his profile.

Highlights of the curriculum:

Essential (the core to fix his #1 weakness — 32% Black win rate from an inconsistent repertoire):
- Lifetime Repertoires: Giri's Najdorf Sicilian (Chessable, ~$99.99) — the consolidation course, with a 1.5-hr Quickstart and 921 drillable lines to build automatic recall for blitz.
- Power Play 18: The Sicilian Najdorf (Daniel King, ChessBase, ~$27) — structure-first teaching to fix his middlegame-transition gap; the gentle first exposure.

Recommended:
- Opening Repertoire: The Sicilian Najdorf (Doknjas, Everyman, ~$27) — 42 model games pitched exactly at 1800-2200.
- Free YouTube Najdorf masterclass game study — trains king-safety judgement (weakness #2) at zero cost.
- Lifetime Repertoires: Kan Sicilian (Chessable, ~$49.99) — lower-theory fallback #1.

Optional / future:
- Cheparinov's Najdorf (Chessable, ~$129.99) — sharper upgrade path for 2200+ on his title journey (pick this OR Giri, not both).
- GM Repertoire 6: The Sicilian Defence (Ftacnik, Quality Chess, ~$33) — deep reference.
- Harikrishna's Taimanov (Chessable, ~$99.99) — lower-theory alternative #2, with the trade-off vs the Kan explained.

Methodology note: Chessable course pages render as SPA shells and could not be scraped directly via WebFetch, so prices reflect Chessable's standard tiers confirmed across search snippets and the publisher pattern (Lifetime Repertoires premium ~$99.99-129.99; Kan ~$49.99 confirmed in search). King's Power Play 18 and the print books are verified via ChessBase shop and Amazon listings. The two lower-theory alternatives include the explicit Najdorf-vs-Kan/Taimanov trade-off as requested.
